questions.prompt: What do the three blocks of the Arrange-Act-Assert pattern do?
options: Arrange compiles, Act deploys, Assert reports
Arrange sets up state and data, Act performs the action under test, Assert verifies the outcome
Arrange logs in, Act waits, Assert retries
Arrange and Act are optional; only Assert matters
questions.explanation: Arrange prepares state and data, Act performs the single action being tested, and Assert checks the result. The structure makes the test read top to bottom like a sentence.

questions.prompt: Why should a single test focus on one behaviour?
options: Tests with one assertion run on a different thread
A focused failure points directly at the cause, while a test full of unrelated assertions stops at the first failure and hides the rest
TestNG only allows one assertion per test
It makes the test file shorter, which is the main goal
questions.explanation: One behaviour per test means a red result is a diagnosis. A test crammed with unrelated checks stops at the first failed assertion, so you never learn what else broke.

questions.prompt: Which test name best communicates intent in a failure report?
questions.explanation: A good name states the scenario and the expected result, so a failing name in the report tells you what broke without opening the code.

questions.prompt: Why must each test be independent of the others?
options: So tests can be deleted at random
So each one passes on its own and in any order, instead of relying on data or state another test left behind
Independence only matters for manual tests
questions.explanation: If test B needs test A to have run first, reordering or running B alone breaks it. Independent tests pass in any order, which prevents a whole class of flaky failures.
